summary: ApoptoSENS project summary describing a CAR-NK cell therapy engineered to target senescent cells using novel senescence-specific surface markers (SENS-1, SENS-2, SENS-3). The study has been completed with a published paper. The project identifies novel markers in IMR-90 cells, develops monoclonal antibodies, and constructs CAR-NK cells for selective senolytic therapy.

content_blocks.title: Project Overview & Scientific Foundation
content_blocks.content: Project Title: ApoptoSENS (study finished, paper published). Therapeutic Concept: Develops CAR-NK cells engineered to selectively recognize and eliminate senescent cells that accumulate with age and drive chronic inflammation, tissue dysfunction, and aging-related disease. The team identifies novel senescence-specific surface markers, validates them across models, generates monoclonal antibodies, and uses them to build CAR-NK constructs with improved precision and safety over existing senolytics.

content_blocks.title: Target Indication and Patient Population
content_blocks.content: Target Indication: The therapy targets the accumulation of senescent cells. Patient Population Size: Not specified.

content_blocks.title: Mechanism of Action
content_blocks.content: CAR-NK cells are engineered to recognize senescence-specific surface antigens (SENS-1, SENS-2, SENS-3) that are absent on healthy cells. Upon binding the target, NK cells induce apoptosis in senescent cells, reducing SASP-driven inflammation and tissue dysfunction.

content_blocks.title: Biological Target
content_blocks.content: Novel senescence-associated surface markers discovered in IMR-90 cells rendered senescent by ionizing radiation or doxorubicin. Top candidates include SENS-1, SENS-2, SENS-3, with SENS-3 showing the strongest early promise. These markers are identified as senescence-specific surface antigens absent on healthy cells.

content_blocks.title: Current Development Stage
content_blocks.content: Study finished, paper published. This indicates completion of initial research phase with peer-reviewed publication of results.

content_blocks.title: Development Timeline & Milestones
content_blocks.content: Next 3 major milestones: Not specified. Current status noted as 'study finished, paper published'.

content_blocks.title: Team Information
content_blocks.content: Key Personnel: Dr. Amit Sharma, Project Lead, SENS Research Foundation. Affiliations: SENS Research Foundation, Senescence Immunology Research Group. Dr. Sharma holds PhD from University of Pune with prior postdoctoral research at Buck Institute. Team Expertise Areas: Cellular senescence biology, Immunology and immune aging, NK-cell and CAR-cell engineering, Target discovery and antibody generation, Translational aging-related therapeutics.

content_blocks.title: Intellectual Property Status
content_blocks.content: Patent Application Status: Not specified. Scope of IP Protection: Not specified (fields for composition of matter, method of use, target claims are present but unfilled). Freedom-to-Operate Assessment: Not specified (no known blocking patents or FTO concerns mentioned).

content_blocks.title: Model System and Experimental Approach
content_blocks.content: Experimental model: IMR-90 cells (human fibroblast cell line) rendered senescent by two methods: ionizing radiation or doxorubicin treatment. This represents an in vitro cell-based model system for identifying senescence-specific markers. The approach involves marker identification, validation across models, monoclonal antibody generation, and CAR-NK construct development.

key_findings: Study completed with published paper - represents completion of initial research phase
Three novel senescence-specific surface markers identified: SENS-1, SENS-2, SENS-3, with SENS-3 showing strongest early promise
Markers discovered using IMR-90 cells rendered senescent by ionizing radiation or doxorubicin - in vitro model system
CAR-NK cell constructs developed using monoclonal antibodies against senescence markers
Team based at SENS Research Foundation with expertise in senescence biology, NK-cell engineering, and antibody generation
Mechanism: CAR-NK cells recognize senescence-specific antigens absent on healthy cells and induce apoptosis
IP status not specified - no information on patent filings or freedom-to-operate
No quantitative efficacy data, animal model validation, or clinical data provided in this summary document
Development milestones beyond publication not specified
